_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Nicollet County, MN. Record survival, courthouse history — St. Peter as failed state capital, Minnesota River county, Gustavus Adolphus College records.

current_courthouse.location: St. Peter, MN — 501 South Minnesota Avenue

previous_courthouses.location: St. Peter, various temporary locations including commercial buildings
previous_courthouses.fate: County government housed in rented quarters during boom period.
Replaced after being damaged or outgrown.

previous_courthouses.location: St. Peter, brick courthouse on Minnesota Avenue
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by 1939 WPA-built courthouse. Records transferred intact.

record_losses.description: Nicollet County's location on the Minnesota River floodplain has exposed its records to periodic flood risk. Major floods in 1881, 1919, and 1965 affected parts of St. Peter. Some records stored in basement vaults suffered water damage over the years, though the bound volumes were generally preserved. The 1939 WPA courthouse was designed with better flood protection. The most significant damage was to loose papers and some early court files stored at lower elevations.
record_losses.narrative_impact: If {ancestor_name}'s records in Nicollet County have gaps, the Minnesota River's periodic flooding is a possible cause — especially for loose papers and files that were not in bound volumes.
record_losses.source: Neill, Edward D. History of the Minnesota Valley. Minneapolis: North Star Publishing, 1882.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county organization during the St. Peter land boom
deeds.gaps: No known major gaps. Early deeds reflect the speculative boom of 1856-1857 when St. Peter expected to become the state capital.
deeds.repository: Nicollet County Recorder, St. Peter; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm
deeds.online_access: FamilySearch microfilm; Ancestry.com MN land records

wills_estates.completeness: Good — probate records from early settlement
wills_estates.gaps: Some early estate files may have water damage from flood exposure.
wills_estates.repository: Nicollet County Probate Court, St. Peter; Minnesota Historical Society

marriages.completeness: Good — marriage records from early organization
marriages.gaps: Pre-1870 marriages performed by clergy were not always recorded civilly. Scandinavian and German immigrant church marriages supplement the civil record.
marriages.repository: Nicollet County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society microfilm

court_minutes.completeness: Good — district court minutes from county organization
court_minutes.gaps: Some early justice of the peace records may be incomplete or water-damaged.
court_minutes.repository: Nicollet County Courthouse; Minnesota Historical Society

vital_records.completeness: Compliance was uneven until the 1890s. St. Peter city records may be more complete than rural township records.
vital_records.repository: Minnesota Department of Health; Nicollet County Recorder; Minnesota Historical Society

naturalization_records.completeness: Good — Nicollet County's mixed immigrant population (Scandinavian, German, Yankee) generated steady naturalization filings
naturalization_records.gaps: Some immigrants filed initial papers in transit counties.
naturalization_records.repository: Nicollet County District Court; National Archives — Chicago; Minnesota Historical Society

substitute_sources.description: Norwegian, Swedish, and German Lutheran congregations in Nicollet County maintained parish registers. Gustavus Adolphus College (founded 1862 in St. Peter by Swedish Lutherans) also holds institutional records that document the Swedish community.
substitute_sources.repository: Norwegian-American Historical Association, Northfield; Swenson Swedish Immigration Research Center; Gustavus Adolphus College Archives, St. Peter; Luther Seminary Archives; FamilySearch microfilm
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Church records are the primary vital records substitute before 1870. The Gustavus Adolphus College archives contain records of the Swedish Lutheran community that extend beyond the college itself.
substitute_sources.source: Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.

substitute_sources.description: Federal schedules 1850–1940 and Minnesota state/territorial censuses. The 1857 territorial census captures the St. Peter boom-era population.
substitute_sources.repository: National Archives; FamilySearch; Ancestry.com; Minnesota Historical Society
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: The 1857 territorial census is critical for the speculative-boom population. Census records document the county's mixed ethnic character — Yankees, Scandinavians, and Germans in adjacent townships.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.

substitute_sources.description: The St. Peter Tribune (founded 1859) and successor papers published legal notices, obituaries, land transactions, and local news. As a county seat paper, it carried court proceedings and estate notices.
substitute_sources.repository: Minnesota Historical Society; Nicollet County Historical Society; microfilm at various libraries
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: County seat newspapers are invaluable for supplementing courthouse records — obituaries, marriage announcements, and legal notices may provide details not found in the official record.
substitute_sources.source: Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Newspapers Index.

narrative_hooks: Nicollet County's records reach back to 1854 — the boom year when St. Peter's founders expected it to become the state capital — and if {ancestor_name} bought land during the speculative frenzy, the deed still exists in the St. Peter courthouse.

The Minnesota River that made St. Peter possible also threatened its records — periodic flooding damaged some early loose papers, which means that {ancestor_name}'s formal record trail may have gaps caused not by neglect but by water.
If {ancestor_name} was part of the Swedish Lutheran community in Nicollet County, the Gustavus Adolphus College archives in St. Peter may hold community records that supplement the courthouse files.

Before Minnesota required vital registration in 1870, the church baptismal register was the birth certificate — and in Nicollet County's mixed community, those records might be in Norwegian, Swedish, German, or English depending on which congregation the family attended.
source_references: Neill, Edward D. History of the Minnesota Valley. Minneapolis: North Star Publishing, 1882.
Qualey, Carlton C. Norwegian Settlement in the United States. Northfield, MN: Norwegian-American Historical Association, 1938.
Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Census Records Guide. St. Paul: MHS, 2004.
Minnesota Historical Society. Minnesota Newspapers Index.
